coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots from Shewanella oneidensis MR–1 for Hg2+ and tetracycline detection and selective fluorescence imaging of Gram–positive bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Chengliang
Dong, Chen
Cheng, Lei
Shi, Xianyang
Bi, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Mercury ion (Hg2+) and tetracycline are environmental pollutants that endanger the ecological environment and human health. In this work, fluorescent carbon dots (denoted CDs@MR–1) were synthesized via a one–step hydrothermal process using Shewanella oneidensis MR–1. The prepared CDs@MR–1 detected Hg2+ and tetracycline in real water samples. The fluorescence quenching efficiency showed a good linear relationship between the range of 0–9.38 μg·mL–1 (R 2 = 0.997) and 0–7.5 μg·mL–1 (R 2 = 0.995), and the limits of detection were 0.43 μg·mL−1 and 0.21 μg·mL−1 for Hg2+ and tetracycline, respectively. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching can be attributed to the internal filtration effect (IFE) for tetracycline, whereas dynamic quenching (DQ), static quenching (SQ), and the IFE contribute to the Hg2+ detection. Additionally, the obtained CDs@MR–1 has been shown to selectively interact with Gram–positive bacteria and distinguish Gram–positive bacteria from Gram–negative bacteria. The multifunctionality of CDs@MR–1 can be useful in various applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Shewanella oneidensis MR–1
Carbon dots
Hg2+
Tetracycline
Fluorescence probe
Bacteria differentiation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of Hg2+ and tetracycline
Distinguishing between Gram–negative and Gram–positive bacteria
Selectivity of Hg2+ and tetracycline detection
Sensitivity of Hg2+ and tetracycline detection
Possible mechanism of Hg2+ and tetracycline detection
Determination of Hg2+ and tetracycline in real water samples
Selective fluorescence imaging of Gram–positive bacteria

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the formation mechanism of CDs from bacterial cells.
TEM image (a), particle size distribution diagram (b), XRD image (c), and FT–IR spectra (d) of CDs@MR–1.
(a) XPS spectrum and high–resolution (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s spectra of CDs@MR–1.
(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs@MR–1 (Inset: photos of CDs@MR–1 under visible light (left) and UV light (right)), and (b) excitation dependent emission spectra of CDs@MR–1.
Effect of pH (a), illumination time (b), NaCl concentration, (c) and resting period (d) on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MR–1.
Fluorescence quenching efficiency of common metal ions (a) and aromatic compounds/antibiotics (b) toward CDs@MR–1.
Effect of sample pH on the detection of (a) Hg2+ and (b) tetracycline. Effect of reaction time on the detection of (c) Hg2+ and (d) tetracycline.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs@MR–1 at various Hg2+ (0–800 μg·mL–1) (a) and tetracycline (0 −80 μg·mL–1) (c) concentrations. Linear relationship between F/F0 and Hg2+ (0–9.38 μg·mL–1) (b), and tetracycline (0–7.5 μg·mL–1) (d) concentrations (3δ/k, δ: Standard deviation for blank samples, n = 11, k: The slope of the standard curve).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs@MR–1, Hg2+, and CDs@MR–1 + Hg2+ (Inset: photographs of CDs@MR–1 before (left) and after (right) adding Hg2+ ions); (b) CDs@MR–1, tetracycline, and CDs@MR–1 + tetracycline (Inset: photographs of CDs@MR–1 before (left) and after (right) adding tetracycline); (c) FL decay lifetime of CDs@MR–1 and CDs@MR–1 + Hg2+; (d) FL decay lifetime of CDs@MR–1 and CDs@MR–1 + tetracycline; (e) UV–vis absorption of Hg2+ and fluorescence spectra of CDs@MR–1; (f) UV–vis absorption of tetracycline.
Confocal images of two strains of Gram–positive bacteria (S.aureus and B.subtilis) and two strains of Gram–negative bacteria (E.coli and P.aeruginosa) after treatment with CDs@MR–1 for 2 h.
Detection of Hg2+ in tap water and lake water samples.
Detection of tetracycline in tap water and lake water samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots from Shewanella oneidensis MR–1 for Hg2+ and tetracycline detection and selective fluorescence imaging of Gram–positive bacteria

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Wetland Ecosystem Protection and Restoration, School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui, 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Wetland Ecosystem Protection and Restoration, School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Anhui University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Wetland Ecosystem Protection and Restoration, School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui, 230601, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury ion (Hg2+) and tetracycline are environmental pollutants that endanger the ecological environment and human health. In this work, fluorescent carbon dots (denoted CDs@MR–1) were synthesized via a one–step hydrothermal process using Shewanella oneidensis MR–1. The prepared CDs@MR–1 detected Hg2+ and tetracycline in real water samples. The fluorescence quenching efficiency showed a good linear relationship between the range of 0–9.38 μg·mL–1 (R 2 = 0.997) and 0–7.5 μg·mL–1 (R 2 = 0.995), and the limits of detection were 0.43 μg·mL−1 and 0.21 μg·mL−1 for Hg2+ and tetracycline, respectively. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching can be attributed to the internal filtration effect (IFE) for tetracycline, whereas dynamic quenching (DQ), static quenching (SQ), and the IFE contribute to the Hg2+ detection. Additionally, the obtained CDs@MR–1 has been shown to selectively interact with Gram–positive bacteria and distinguish Gram–positive bacteria from Gram–negative bacteria. The multifunctionality of CDs@MR–1 can be useful in various applications.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of modern industry and medicine, many heavy metal ions and antibiotics are discarded into the aquatic environment [1,2]. Mercury ions (Hg2+), is a persistent, non–biodegradable and highly toxic pollutant, threating the water environment and human health. Exposure to a high level of mercury damages the kidney, brain, endocrine system, and central nervous system in body's somatic cells [3–5]. Similarly, while antibiotics such as tetracycline are used to treat human and animal diseases and boost agricultural production, their abuse causes environmental and health problems. Previous studies have shown that the incomplete metabolism of tetracycline can be detected in surface water, groundwater, and hospital wastewater. Tetracycline residues in the aquatic environment are bio–toxic and resistant, causing hepatic and kidney damage, allergic reactions, gastrointestinal reactions, and yellow teeth [6,7]. Therefore, developing a simple and effective method for monitoring Hg2+ and tetracycline in the environment is particularly important [8,9].

Over the past decades, various methods have been used to detect Hg2+ and tetracycline, such as atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, capillary electrophoresis, high–performance liquid chromatography, electrochemical analysis, and colorimetric detection. However, most of these methods have disadvantages that limit their environmental applications, including complicated sample pretreatment, expensive equipment, low reliability, time–consuming analysis, and secondary pollutant generation [10–15]. Recently, a low cost, eco–friendly, and solvent–free method using carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probes has been developed for detecting environmental pollutants and can effectively solve these problems. CDs stand out among carbon nanomaterials due to their excellent properties such as ultrafine particle size (<10 nm), simple preparation, low toxicity, and biocompatibility [16–19]. Compared with traditional fluorescent probes based on metallic quantum dots, organic dyes, polymers, and fluorescent metal nanoparticles, CDs overcome easy oxidation, high toxicity, and low quantum yield and show great potential in bioimaging, catalysis, drug delivery, and sensing. However, harsh and impractical conditions are required during the synthesis of CDs from chemical sources, such as the need for strong acids or bases as passivators, expensive or toxic solvents, complex operations, or strict control of the reaction process. Therefore, it is urgent to develop an alternative, environmentally friendly, and economical approach for the synthesis of CDs.

Various fluorescent CDs have been synthesized from rosin, paper, fruit, mushroom, honey, food waste, and even human urine and hair [20–27]. In general, such CDs show more stable and strong fluorescence due to the presence of nitrogen and phosphorus in biomass. As a ubiquitous microorganism, bacteria are widely distributed in nature, and scholars have successfully extracted CDs from Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Rhizobium, and other bacteria [13,28,29]. However, the use of CDs as sensors, have not been thoroughly studied. For example, Zhang et al. reported CDs synthesized from Bacillus cereus for the detection of only p–nitrophenol [30]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop multifunctional bacterial CDs that can detect a variety of pollutants.

In this work, S. oneidensis MR–1, which is a typical dissimilatory metal reducing bacterium, widely distributed in the environment due to its ability to reduce metal ions, and is a low-cost carbon source, was used to prepare fluorescent CDs (CDs@MR–1) via a simple one–step hydrothermal process. The stability of the obtained CDs@MR–1 from bacteria was evaluated under a wide pH range, high salinity, UV illumination, and various storage conditions. Based on different quenching mechanisms, CDs@MR–1 was used to detect Hg2+ and tetracycline both in simulated and real water. Furthermore, the feasibility of using CDs@MR–1 to distinguish Gram–positive bacteria from Gram–negative bacteria was investigated.

Chloramphenicol, sulfadiazine, and sulfamethazine were purchased from J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. Erythromycin and sulfamethoxazole were obtained from TCI (Shanghai) Development Co., Ltd, and Sigma Co., Ltd, respectively. Tetracycline was obtained from Toronto Research Chemicals Co., Ltd. Methylbenzene, aniline,1,4–dichlorobenzene,3–nitrochlorobenzene,4–nitrochlorobenzene,1,3–dinitrobenzene, phenol, p–chlorophenol, and 2,4,6–Trichlorophenol were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai) Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Inorganic salts were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemical substances were analytical grade. Ultrapure water (Milli–Q system, 18.25 MΩ cm, 25 °C) was used for all aqueous stock solutions.

Luria–Bertani (LB) medium was prepared for 5 g peptones (Oxoid, Hampshire, UK), 2.5 g yeast extract (Oxoid, Hampshire, UK), and 5 g NaCl to form a 0.5–L aqueous solution, which was adjusted to pH 7.0. The LB liquid stock solution was used to incubate bacterial strains. Two Gram–positive bacteria, S. aureus and B. subtilis, and three Gram–negative bacteria, S. oneidensis MR–1, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and E. coli were used. All testing apparatus were sterilized at high temperature (121 °C, 20 min).

The morphology, crystal structure, element composition, and functional group analysis of CDs@MR–1 were performed using JM–2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan), SmartLab9KW X–ray diffraction (Rigaku, Japan), ESCALAB 250Xi X–ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA), and Vertex80 +Hyperion2000 Fourier Infrared Microscopy System (BRUKER OPTICS, Germany), respectively. The optical properties were characterized by F–4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan) and U–4100 UV–vis/NIR spectrometer (HITACHI, Japan).

CDs@MR–1 were prepared via a one–step hydrothermal method from S. oneidensis MR–1 cell. Briefly, S. oneidensis MR–1 was cultured in 500 mL LB liquid media at 30 °C for 24 h with shaking at 150 rpm. Subsequently, the supernatant was removed by centrifugation (4 °C, 6000 rpm, and 5 min). The precipitate was washed three times with Milli–Q water, centrifuged (4 °C, 6000 rpm, 5 min), and then resuspended in 50 mL Milli–Q water. The bacterial culture suspension was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon–lined stainless–steel autoclave and heated for 12 h at 180 °C until the reaction completed. After cooled to room temperature, large particles and aggregates were removed by centrifugation (4 °C, 9000 rpm, 15 min) and filtration (0.22 µm filters, Millipore). CDs@MR–1 were stored at 4 °C until further analysis.

The absolute quantum yield (QY) was calculated using the following formula：
(1)
Q
CD
s
=
E
c
-
E
a
L
a
-
L
c
where Q
CDs is the QY of the prepared CDs@MR–1, E is the integral area of the emission peak (λ
em =410 nm), and L represents the integral area of the absorption peak (λ
ex =320 nm). The subscripts c and a indicate the prepared CDs@MR–1 and blank samples (Milli–Q water), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of Hg2+ and tetracycline
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of Hg2+ and tetracycline, stock solutions of fluorescent CDs@MR–1 (40 μL), Milli–Q water (1.76 mL), and 200 μL Hg2+/tetracycline samples with different concentrations were added to 4–mL polypropylene centrifuge tubes. After mixing for 5 min, their fluorescence spectra (FL) were acquired at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The selectivity of the fluorescent CDs@MR–1 toward Hg2+ and tetracycline was calculated by adding other potential interference substances to the solutions instead of Hg2+ and tetracycline as described above. The steady-state photoluminescence spectra (PL) were recorded using an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm. The experiments were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Distinguishing between Gram–negative and Gram–positive bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Two strains of Gram–negative bacteria and two strains of Gram–positive bacteria were used for evaluating the imaging performance of CDs@MR–1. After the bacteria were cultured to the logarithmic growth stage, they were harvested and washed three times with phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (4000 rpm, 3 min) and resuspended. The 100 μL bacterial suspension (107～108 CFU mL–1) was mixed with the CDs solution of the same volume. After incubation for 2 h (37 °C, 200 rpm), the unreacted CDs were removed by washing three times with PBS (4000 rpm, 3 min), and then imaging was performed using a confocal laser scanning microscope (TCS SP8 DIVE, Leica, Germany).

As a type of biomass consisting of water, inorganic salts, proteins, carbohydrates, lipids, and nucleic acids, bacteria can also be used to synthesize CDs. First, bacterial cells (S. oneidensis MR–1) are pyrolyzed at high temperatures to obtain large molecules, such as proteins, polysaccharides, and lipids. Subsequently, these macromolecules are further decomposed peptides, amino acids, hydrocarbons, and other small molecules. Meanwhile, small molecules are partially polymerized and then carbonized into CDs via the continuous hydrothermal treatment [31]. The possible mechanism for the formation of CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1.

The morphology, structure, surface functionalities, and chemical composition of CDs@MR–1 were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X–ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier–transform infrared (FT–IR) spectroscopy, and X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). CDs@MR–1 were monodisperse, nearly spherical with an average diameter of 3.95 nm ( Fig. 2a and b). The XRD pattern (Fig. 2c) reveals a broad diffraction peak at approximately 22°, which corresponds to the (002) plane of the disordered carbons of CDs@MR–1 [32]. FT–IR spectroscopy was used to reveal the functional groups on the surface of CDs@MR–1 (Fig. 2d). The bands at 3387 and 2926 cm−1 in the FT–IR spectrum of CDs@MR–1 correspond to the O–H and C–H bending vibrations [33]. The broad absorption band around 3200 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of O–H and/or N–H. In addition, the peak obtained at 1674 cm–1 results from the CO stretching vibrations of the amide (–CONH–) or carboxyl (COOH–) bonds [30]. The CC stretching vibration peak was found at 1630 cm–1, and a distinct characteristic peak at 1592 cm–1 was labeled as the stretching vibration of C–N [34]. Two characteristic peaks at 1347 cm–1 and 1075 cm–1 originated from the asymmetric stretching vibrations of the C–O bond and the stretching vibrations of the C–H bond [35].

The components and surface functionalities were characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 3a, C, N, O, S, and P were detected in CDs@MR–1 with binding energies of 285, 400, 532, 162.89 eV, and 132.94 eV, respectively. The high–resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3b) gave two CC peaks at 284 eV and C–C/C–H peak at 284.85 eV related to the graphitic and amorphous structures, and three other carbon species, C–O at 286.6 eV, O–CO at 288.15 eV, and CO at 288.93 eV [12,36,37]. The high–resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 3c) corresponds to two different chemical environments, N–H at 399.2 eV and CN–C at 399.95 eV, respectively [38]. Additionally, the high–resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) revealed three peaks at 531.25, 532.35 and 533.2 eV due to the presence of CO, C–OH/C–O–C and –COOH [39]. These XPS results agreed with the FT–IR analyses and showed that the as–synthesized CDs@MR–1 contained C, H, O, N, P, and S. The obtained CDs@MR–1 was rich in oxygen–containing functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH2, and –CONH–), resulting in good water solubility based.

The optical properties of CDs were evaluated with UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 4a, UV–vis absorption spectra displayed an absorption peak at 258 nm and 320 nm, respectively, which can be attributed to the π–π * transition of CC and the n–π * transition of CO [40]. The suspension of CDs@MR–1 showed a light–yellow color under sunlight irradiation and turned to green–blue after UV excitation, indicating that fluorescent CDs have been successfully prepared (inset of Fig. 4a). The QY of CDs@MR–1 was measured to be 7.0% using combined time–resolved & steady–state fluorescence (Fluoromax–4, China). Besides, Fig. 4a shows the maximum emission peak at 410 nm at the optimal excitation wavelength of 320 nm. Generally, CDs typically present excitation–dependent PL behavior. With the excitation wavelength varied from 280 nm to 360 nm, the fluorescent peaks red–shifted from 380 nm to 440 nm (Fig. 4c), caused by the different emission sites and uneven particle size on the surface of CDs@MR–1.

The effects of pH value, UV irradiation time, salt concentration and storage time on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MR–1 were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5a, the obtained CDs@MR–1 exhibited low fluorescence intensity both at low pH (<4) and high pH (>12) and reached the maximum value at pH 12. However, the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged from pH 4 to pH 11. The reason for the drastic decrease in fluorescence intensity in the strong acid or base environments may be that too many H+ or OH− ions induce significant changes in functional groups, disrupting or even preventing the electronic transitions of some defects [41]. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of CDs may decrease dramatically due to their aggregation under acidic conditions [42]. The effects of ionic strength and illumination time on the fluorescence intensity are shown in Fig. 5b and c. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity hardly changed even at high NaCl concentration (2 mmol·L–1) and extended exposure (4 h). The fluorescence intensity of CDs@MR–1 only slightly decreased after two months of storage time according to Fig. 5d. These results prove the fluorescence stability of CDs@MR–1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of Hg2+ and tetracycline detection
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of CDs@MR–1–13 metal ions, 9 aromatic compounds, and 6 antibiotics was investigated. The fluorescence intensity of different possible interfering substances was monitored, such as Cu2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, Co2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, methylbenzene, 1,4–dichlorobenzene,3–nitrochlorobenzene, 4–nitrochlorobenzene, 1,3–dinitrobenzene, phenol, p–chlorophenol, 2,4,6–trichlorophenol, aniline, chloramphenicol, sulfadiazine, sulfamethazine, erythromycin, sulfamethoxazole, and Tetracycline. As shown in Fig. 6a and b, only Hg2+ and tetracycline effectively quenched the fluorescence of CDs@MR–1, indicating that CDs@MR–1 can act as a fluorescent probe with high selectivity toward Hg2+ and tetracycline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of Hg2+ and tetracycline detection
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of pH and incubation time on quenching efficiency of Hg2+ and tetracycline were investigated. In the pH range 2–12, the fluorescence quenching efficiency first decreased (pH 2–4), then increased (pH 4–10) and then decreased (pH 10–12), and reached the maximum value at pH 10. Thus, pH 10 was the optimum pH value for Hg2+ detection ( Fig. 7a). The fluorescence quenching efficiency gradually decreased for tetracycline detection in the pH range 2–12 (Fig. 7b). However, the fluorescence of CDs@MR–1 may be quenched due to self–aggregation when the pH value was less than 4 (Fig. 5a). Hence, we set the detection condition of tetracycline at pH 4. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@MR–1 with Hg2+ and tetracycline at 50 μg·mL–1 for different incubation periods was performed to investigate the stability of the bacteria–derived CDs. The fluorescence intensity of CDs within 30 min was investigated after adding Hg2+ and tetracycline. The fluorescence intensity remained stable after 5 min of incubation, so 5 min was chosen as the optimal fluorescence quenching time for Hg2+ (Fig. 7c). Similarly, 3 min was considered as the optimal fluorescence quenching time for tetracycline (Fig. 7d) because the fluorescence intensity of CDs@MR–1 hardly changed after 3 min of tetracycline addition. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the relationship between the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs@MR–1 from bacteria and the concentration of Hg2+ or tetracycline was studied, and quantitative analysis of fluorescence quenching was established. To assess the sensitivity for the quantitative detection of Hg2+, CDs@MR–1 solution with various concentrations of Hg2+ (0–800 μg·mL–1) or tetracycline (0–80 μg·mL–1) was investigated by recording the FL emission spectra. The PL intensity of the bacteria–derived CDs decreased with the increasing Hg2+/tetracycline concentrations ( Fig. 8a and c), indicating that the FL sensor is very sensitive toward Hg2+ and tetracycline. The plot of F/F0 and the concentrations of Hg2+ ranging from 0 to 9.38 μg·mL–1 exhibited good linearity with a correlation coefficient R2 = 0.997 (Fig. 8b). As shown in Fig. 8d, the ratio of the PL intensity was linear with tetracycline concentration (0–7.5 μg·mL–1, R2 = 0.999). The detection limits of Hg2+ and tetracycline were 0.43 μg·mL–1 and 0.21 μg·mL–1, respectively. These results confirmed that CDs@MR–1 can be used as a nanoprobe for the detection of Hg2+ and tetracycline.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of Hg2+ and tetracycline detection
ce:section.ce:para: There are five types of CD fluorescence quenching mechanism: dynamic quenching (DQ), static quenching (SQ), photoinduced electron transfer, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and internal filtration effect (IFE) [43]. To study the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Hg2+ and tetracycline on CDs@MR–1, we analyzed the UV–visible absorption spectra of CDs@MR–1 with and without Hg2+ and tetracycline. Generally, SQ changes the UV–visible absorption spectrum of CDs because of the formation of new substances. As shown in Fig. 9a, the characteristic absorption peak of the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs@MR–1 almost disappeared after the addition of Hg2+ ions, indicating that Hg2+ ions can influence the surface functional groups of CDs@MR–1 [39]. –COOH, –OH, CONH–, and C–O–C groups were detected on the surface of the CDs@MR–1 by FT–IR and XPS, and the FL quenching may be due to the stable complexes formed by strong binding affinity and fast chelating at the surface of CDs@MR–1 [44,45]. This can further be inferred from the inset in Fig. 9a by the presence of a yellow precipitate in the CDs@MR–1 solution after the addition of Hg2+ ions. However, we did not find significant changes in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs@MR–1 and in the presence of tetracycline, indicating that no new substance was produced in the mixture of CDs@MR–1 and tetracycline (inset of Fig. 9b). Therefore, the possibility of tetracycline quenching the fluorescence of CDs@MR–1 caused by SQ can be eliminated. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs@MR–1 in the absence or presence of Hg2+ and tetracycline were also measured. The results showed that the average lifetime decreased without (2.868 ns) or with Hg2+ (2.524 ns), while almost remained unchanged in the absence (2.868 ns) or presence (2.793 ns) of tetracycline in Fig. 9b and c. Since the FRET and DQ mechanisms decrease the donor fluorescence lifetime, it is likely that FRET did not play an important role in the quenching process of the tetracycline CDs@MR–1 system [46]. As for the fluorescence quenching of CDs@MR–1 by Hg2+, the occurrence of this phenomenon can be attributed to SQ and DQ. When Hg2+ ions were added to the CDs@MR–1 solution, the collision of Hg2+ with each other happened, producing DQ. Then the surface groups of CDs@MR–1 and Hg2+ were successfully combined, resulting in the SQ and the disappearance of fluorescence [47].

Considering the competitive absorption and mutual masking of light, we compared the absorption spectra of Hg2+ and tetracycline with the excitation or emission spectra of CDs@MR–1. As shown in Fig. 9e, the absorption spectrum of Hg2+ overlapped with the excitation spectrum of CDs@MR–1, suggesting that Hg2+ may compete to absorb the excitation light of CDs@MR–1 and reduce the fluorescence. For tetracycline, the absorption spectrum overlapped not only with the excitation spectrum but also the emission spectrum of the prepared CDs@MR–1 in Fig. 9f. Thus, tetracycline can simultaneously absorb the excitation light and shelter the emission light of CDs@MR–1; consequently, the fluorescence disappeared. However, the absorption of the excitation light or the reabsorption of the emission light by the quencher is not a quenching process but only excitation beam attenuation or the reabsorption of the emitted radiation [48]. In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of tetracycline on CDs@MR–1 was attributed to the IFE [49]. For Hg2+ detection mechanism using CDs@MR–1, the quenching mechanism could be considered as a complicated process caused by the combination of SQ, DQ, and the IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg2+ and tetracycline in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The tap water and lake water were as real water samples to test the feasibility of CDs@MR–1 to detect Hg2+ and tetracycline. Large particles in collected samples were removed by centrifugation (12000 rpm,15 min) and filtration (0.22 µm). The recoveries of Hg2+ and tetracycline in unknown water samples were determined by the standard addition method, and the results are shown in
Tables 1 and 2. The recoveries of all samples ranged from 93.54 to 119.22 and an RSD from 0.21 to 1.66, indicating that the synthesized CDs@MR–1 can be used for the detection of Hg2+ and tetracycline in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective fluorescence imaging of Gram–positive bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria CDs have been used as dyes to stain bacteria [28,29,35]. To test bacterial staining capability of CDs@MR–1, we selected two Gram–positive bacteria (S. aureus and B. subtilis) and two Gram–negative bacteria (P. aeruginosa and E. coli) as the research objects. As shown in Fig. 10, after treatment with CDs@MR–1 for 2 h, the two Gram–positive bacteria showed blue, green, and red fluorescence emission under different excitations, 405, 488, and 552 nm, respectively. However, multicolor fluorescence was observed only for the two Gram–positive bacteria, indicating that the fluorescent CDs@MR–1 can be used for fluorescence imaging–based differentiation of Gram–positive and Gram–negative bacteria [50]. The reasons for this phenomenon may be explained as follows: the ζ potential of CDs@MR–1 (−13.4 mV) was caused by abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs@MR–1, including carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups. Since bacteria are also negatively charged, we speculate that electrostatic interactions were not strong enough for CDs@MR–1 to contact bacteria [40]. It might be related to the structure and chemical attributes of the bacterial cell walls because of the protection, material transport and information transfer functions to bacteria. Compared with the cell wall of Gram–negative bacteria composed of a double–layered membrane, the cell walls of the Gram–positive bacteria consist of a thick but simple single–layered membrane (peptidoglycan and teichoic acids), and these porous cell walls may promote the interaction between CDs@MR–1 and Gram–positive bacteria due to the polar groups on the surface and the small size (average diameter = 3.90 nm) of CDs@MR–1 [50,51]. These results suggest that the prepared CDs@MR–1 can be used to distinguish between Gram–negative and Gram–positive bacteria simply and quickly.

Bacteria cells (S. oneidensis MR–1) were used as a carbon source for the one–step hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent CDs. The synthesized CDs with good aqueous dispersity displayed high selectivity and sensitivity to Hg2+ and tetracycline in real water samples, offering high potential in real world applications. SQ, DQ and the IFE collectively affected the fluorescence quenching of CDs@MR–1 by Hg2+, whereas the IFE dominated in the tetracycline detection mechanism. In addition, the stable and multicolor fluorescence emission properties of CDs enabled them to simultaneously stain and distinguish Gram–type bacteria due to the unique surface and chemical structure of Gram–positive bacteria. Our findings can facilitate the use of bacteria as raw materials and expand the applications of bacteria–derived CDs.

Chengliang Shen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Chen Dong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing-original draft. Lei Cheng: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hong Bi: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Xianyang Shi: Supervision, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundations of China (5178001 and 51772001) and Major Scientific and Technological Projects in Anhui Province (20203a0702012).
